We evaluated ten experimental variants of our proposed method on the validation set, measuring accuracy as the primary metric. Each variant was run with five random seeds, and we report mean accuracy and standard deviation for each configuration. The experimental results are summarized as follows (mean ± std):

- **variant_01**: 67.2 ± 0.8  
- **variant_02**: 66.9 ± 0.7  
- **variant_03 (Baseline, no proposed component)**: 67.8 ± 0.6  
- **variant_04**: 65.1 ± 0.9  
- **variant_05**: 66.3 ± 1.0  
- **variant_06**: 64.7 ± 0.7  
- **variant_07**: 65.8 ± 0.8  
- **variant_08**: 70.4 ± 0.5  
- **variant_09**: 66.5 ± 0.7  
- **variant_10**: 65.9 ± 0.8  

Among the nine proposed variants (variants 01–02 and 04–10), only **variant_08** outperformed the baseline (variant_03). Specifically, variant_08 achieved a validation accuracy of 70.4%, representing a substantial absolute improvement of +2.6 percentage points over the baseline accuracy of 67.8%. The standard deviation for variant_08 (0.5) was also slightly lower than that of the baseline, suggesting both improved performance and stability.

Conversely, the remaining eight proposed variants failed to match the baseline, with accuracies ranging from 64.7% (variant_06) to 67.2% (variant_01). This indicates that most configurations of the proposed modifications did not consistently yield improvements and, in several cases, led to degraded performance. Only variant_01 approached the baseline within 0.6 percentage points, while the rest lagged behind by 0.6–3.1 absolute points.

In summary, while most variants did not surpass the baseline, **variant_08** demonstrates that the proposed method, when appropriately configured, can yield a meaningful and robust gain in validation accuracy.

We evaluated ten validation-set configurations comprising the baseline (variant_03) and nine instantiations of the proposed component. The baseline achieved 67.8 ± 0.6 accuracy (mean ± std over five seeds). Among the proposed variants, only variant_08 exceeded the baseline, reaching 70.4 ± 0.5, i.e., a +2.6 absolute improvement. All other proposed configurations underperformed relative to the baseline: variant_01 67.2 ± 0.8 (−0.6), variant_02 66.9 ± 0.7 (−0.9), variant_04 65.1 ± 0.9 (−2.7), variant_05 66.3 ± 1.0 (−1.5), variant_06 64.7 ± 0.7 (−3.1), variant_07 65.8 ± 0.8 (−2.0), variant_09 66.5 ± 0.7 (−1.3), and variant_10 65.9 ± 0.8 (−1.9).

Variability across seeds was modest, with standard deviations spanning 0.5–1.0; the most stable configuration was variant_08 (±0.5), and the least stable was variant_05 (±1.0). Ordering the configurations by mean accuracy yields: variant_08 > variant_03 (baseline) > variant_01 > variant_02 > variant_09 > variant_05 > variant_10 > variant_07 > variant_04 > variant_06.

Overall, eight of the nine attempted instantiations of the proposed component reduced validation accuracy by 0.6–3.1 points versus the baseline, indicating that the method is highly sensitive to configuration. Nevertheless, the single successful setting (variant_08) delivers a clear and consistent gain that exceeds the observed run-to-run variability. These results suggest that the proposed mechanism has potential but requires precise design choices to realize benefits. Subsequent analyses therefore focus on dissecting variant_08 to identify which elements are responsible for the observed +2.6 improvement and to understand why the remaining configurations degrade performance.
